Header Layout
Prerequisites
Contributions are welcome. Please follow these guidelines.
Skigen is built under the EES standard — a set of engineering constraints that ensure every cycle spent is a cycle of useful computation.
Requirements
CART decision tree classifier using Gini impurity with recursive binary splits.
Combined L1 and L2 regularization via coordinate descent.
K-Means clustering with Lloyd's algorithm and k-means++ initialization.
Brute-force k-nearest neighbors with majority voting (classifier) or averaging (regressor).
Encodes categorical labels to contiguous integer indices [0, n_classes).
L1-regularized linear regression using coordinate descent with soft-thresholding.
Ordinary Least Squares linear regression via ColPivHouseholderQR decomposition.
L2-regularized logistic regression classifier with IRLS solver. Supports binary and multiclass (one-vs-rest) classification.
Feature scaling by dividing each feature by its maximum absolute value. Scales to [-1, 1] with optional clipping.
Evaluation metrics for regression and classification tasks.
Feature scaling to a specified range [min, max] with optional clipping.
Utilities for splitting data and evaluating models.
Row-wise normalization to unit norm (L1, L2, or max). Stateless transformer.
Principal Component Analysis using full SVD with centering and explained variance computation.
Compile-time pipeline chaining transformers and a final estimator. All steps are type-checked at compile time.
Generates polynomial and interaction features up to specified degree.
L2-regularized least squares solved via Cholesky decomposition on the normal equation.
Scaling using median centering and IQR-based scaling, robust to outliers.
Stochastic Gradient Descent linear models for classification (Hinge or Log loss) and regression.
Standardize features by removing the mean and scaling to unit variance.
Dimensionality reduction via truncated SVD without centering. Suitable for sparse matrices.

Energy-efficient machine learning, native to C++ and Eigen. Skigen is a header-only C++23 library that brings the scikit-learn API to the Eigen ecosystem. It is designed for deployments where compute and memory are scarce resources — embedded systems, real-time pipelines, edge devices, and any environment where interpreter overhead is unacceptable.

Python's scikit-learn is the gold standard for machine learning research and prototyping. It is well-designed, well-documented, and universally understood. Skigen does not aim to replace it — it aims to provide a native execution path for the same algorithms, so that models developed in Python can be deployed in C++ with zero translation loss. scikit-learn Skigen Language Python (NumPy/SciPy backend) C++23 (Eigen backend) API fit() / transform() / predict() fit() / transform() / predict() Overhead Interpreter + GIL + object model Zero — compiles to SIMD instructions Precision float64 default Templatized (double default, float for 2× SIMD density) Memory Copies by default Zero-copy via Eigen::Ref, in-place variants

Eigen-native: Headers, namespaces, and include patterns mirror Eigen conventions. If you know Eigen, Skigen feels like home. Header-only: Drop Skigen/ next to Eigen/ and you're done. No compiled libraries, no link-time dependencies. Bit-level parity: Results match scikit-learn for identical inputs and default parameters. CRTP, not virtual: Static polymorphism via the Curiously Recurring Template Pattern — zero vtable overhead.

#include <Eigen/Dense> #include <Skigen/Preprocessing> #include <iostream> int main() { Eigen::MatrixXd X(4, 2); X << 1, 10, 2, 20, 3, 30, 4, 40; Skigen::StandardScaler scaler; Eigen::MatrixXd Z = scaler.fit_transform(X); std::cout << "Standardized:\n" << Z << "\n"; return 0; }

Skigen follows the same directory convention as Eigen: Skigen/ ├── Core # Module header (no extension) ├── Preprocessing # Module header ├── Dense # Convenience header — bundles all modules └── src/ ├── Core/ │ ├── Traits.h # EigenTypes<Scalar> type aliases │ ├── Concepts.h # C++23 concepts (TransformerLike, PredictorLike) │ ├── Base.h # CRTP bases (Estimator, Transformer, Predictor) │ ├── Validation.h # Input validation │ └── EigenHelpers.h # Numerical utilities └── Preprocessing/ └── StandardScaler.h Users include Skigen exactly like Eigen: #include <Eigen/Dense> // Eigen #include <Skigen/Dense> // Skigen — same pattern #include <Skigen/Core> // or individual modules #include <Skigen/Preprocessing>
All estimators use the Curiously Recurring Template Pattern for zero-cost abstractions: No virtual functions. No vtable. The compiler resolves all dispatch at compile time.
All code lives under namespace Skigen. Internal helpers live under namespace Skigen::internal.
C++23 compiler (GCC ≥ 13, Clang ≥ 17, or MSVC ≥ 19.38) CMake ≥ 3.20 Eigen ≥ 3.4

git clone https://github.com/skigen-project/skigen.git cd skigen cmake -B build \ -DSKIGEN_BUILD_TESTS=ON \ -DSKIGEN_BUILD_EXAMPLES=ON \ -DSKIGEN_BUILD_BENCHMARKS=ON cmake --build build If Eigen is not installed system-wide, place the Eigen source tree as a sibling directory: parent/ ├── eigen/ # Eigen source tree └── skigen/ # This repository CMake will detect it automatically.
./build/tests/skigen_tests
Option Default Description SKIGEN_BUILD_TESTS ON Build the test suite SKIGEN_BUILD_BENCHMARKS OFF Build performance benchmarks SKIGEN_BUILD_EXAMPLES OFF Build example programs
Contributions are welcome. Please follow these guidelines.

C++23. Use modern features (concepts, [[nodiscard]], structured bindings) where they add clarity. No raw loops over matrix elements. Use Eigen expression templates. No virtual. Use CRTP for polymorphism. Warning-free under -Wall -Wextra -Wpedantic.

Create the internal header at Skigen/src/Module/Name.h. Inherit from the appropriate CRTP base (Transformer or Predictor). Implement fit_impl, transform_impl / predict_impl, and accessors. Add the header to the module file (e.g. Skigen/Preprocessing). Write tests in tests/test_name.cpp. Add a parity script in tests/parity/. Document in doc/website/docs/guide/.

Every estimator must have: Unit tests for correctness. A Python parity script that generates reference data from scikit-learn. C++ parity tests that compare Skigen output against the reference. Tolerance: 1e-12 for double, 1e-5 for float.
Keep changes focused — one estimator or feature per PR. Ensure all tests pass before submitting. Include benchmark results if the change affects performance.
Skigen is built under the EES standard — a set of engineering constraints that ensure every cycle spent is a cycle of useful computation.

Machine learning code written in interpreted languages pays a constant overhead for object dispatch, garbage collection, and the GIL. Skigen eliminates this by compiling directly to vectorized machine code.

All estimators are templatized on Scalar (default: double). Switching to float doubles SIMD throughput on the same hardware: Skigen::StandardScaler<float> scaler; // 2× SIMD density Skigen::StandardScaler<double> scaler; // Full precision
All read-only inputs accept Eigen::Ref<const MatrixType>, which supports: Direct matrix references (no copy) Sub-block views Memory-mapped data
Eigen's expression templates fuse operations and vectorize automatically. Skigen never uses manual element-wise loops — all computation flows through Eigen's optimized kernel pipeline.

Every transformer provides _inplace variants that modify data directly, eliminating temporary allocations: scaler.transform_inplace(X); // No allocation Eigen::MatrixXd Z = scaler.transform(X); // Allocates result
CRTP replaces virtual dispatch. The compiler resolves all calls at compile time — no vtable, no indirect jumps, no branch mispredictions from polymorphism.

Throughput: Operations should achieve ≥ 80% of Eigen's raw kernel throughput. Memory: transform_inplace should use zero additional heap memory beyond the input. Float/Double ratio: float throughput should be ≈ 2× double throughput on AVX2 hardware.
C++23 compiler (GCC ≥ 13, Clang ≥ 17, MSVC ≥ 19.38) Eigen ≥ 3.4 CMake ≥ 3.20 (for tests/examples)
Skigen is header-only. Clone it alongside Eigen and point your include path at the repository root: git clone https://github.com/skigen-project/skigen.git
find_package(Eigen3 3.4 REQUIRED) add_subdirectory(skigen) # or use find_package if installed target_link_libraries(my_app PRIVATE Skigen::skigen)
Add both the skigen repo root and your Eigen path to your compiler includes: g++ -std=c++23 -I/path/to/skigen -I/path/to/eigen my_app.cpp -o my_app

#include <Eigen/Dense> #include <Skigen/Dense> #include <iostream> int main() { // Create a dataset Eigen::MatrixXd X(5, 3); X << 1, -1, 2, 2, 0, 0, 0, 1, -1, 1, 1, 1, 3, -1, 0; // Standardize (zero mean, unit variance) Skigen::StandardScaler scaler; Eigen::MatrixXd Z = scaler.fit_transform(X); std::cout << "Standardized:
" << Z << "

"; std::cout << "Mean: " << scaler.mean() << "
"; std::cout << "Scale: " << scaler.scale() << "
"; // Round-trip back to original Eigen::MatrixXd X_back = scaler.inverse_transform(Z); std::cout << "
Recovered:
" << X_back << "
"; return 0; }
cd skigen cmake -B build -DSKIGEN_BUILD_TESTS=ON cmake --build build ./build/tests/skigen_tests
CART decision tree classifier using Gini impurity with recursive binary splits. Mirrors sklearn.tree.DecisionTreeClassifier.

Skigen::DecisionTreeClassifier<Scalar> tree(int max_depth = -1, int min_samples_split = 2); Parameter Default Description max_depth -1 Maximum tree depth (-1 = unlimited) min_samples_split 2 Minimum samples to split a node
Method Description fit(X, y) Build the decision tree predict(X) Predict class labels score(X, y) Return accuracy score
#include <Skigen/Tree> Skigen::DecisionTreeClassifier tree(/*max_depth=*/5); tree.fit(X_train, y_train); std::cout << "Accuracy: " << tree.score(X_test, y_test) << "\n";

Combined L1 and L2 regularization via coordinate descent. min⁡w12n∥Xw−y∥22+α(ρ∥w∥1+1−ρ2∥w∥22)\min_w \frac{1}{2n} \|Xw - y\|_2^2 + \alpha \left( \rho \|w\|_1 + \frac{1-\rho}{2} \|w\|_2^2 \right)minw​2n1​∥Xw−y∥22​+α(ρ∥w∥1​+21−ρ​∥w∥22​) Mirrors sklearn.linear_model.ElasticNet.

Skigen::ElasticNet<Scalar> model(Scalar alpha = 1, Scalar l1_ratio = 0.5, bool fit_intercept = true, int max_iter = 1000, Scalar tol = 1e-4); Parameter Default Description alpha 1 Overall regularization strength l1_ratio 0.5 Mix ratio (1 = Lasso, 0 = Ridge) fit_intercept true Whether to calculate the intercept max_iter 1000 Maximum iterations tol 1e-4 Convergence tolerance
Method Description fit(X, y) Fit the model to training data predict(X) Predict using the linear model score(X, y) Return R² score
#include <Skigen/LinearModel> Skigen::ElasticNet model(/*alpha=*/0.5, /*l1_ratio=*/0.7); model.fit(X, y); auto predictions = model.predict(X_test);
K-Means clustering with Lloyd's algorithm and k-means++ initialization. Mirrors sklearn.cluster.KMeans.

Skigen::KMeans<Scalar> km(int n_clusters = 8, int max_iter = 300, int n_init = 10, unsigned int random_state = 42); Parameter Default Description n_clusters 8 Number of clusters max_iter 300 Maximum iterations per run n_init 10 Number of initializations (best kept) random_state 42 Random seed for reproducibility
Method Description fit(X) Compute k-means clustering predict(X) Predict closest cluster for each sample transform(X) Transform to cluster-distance space fit_predict(X) Fit and return cluster labels

Accessor Type Description cluster_centers() MatrixType Centroid coordinates labels() IndexVector Cluster labels of training data inertia() Scalar Sum of squared distances to centroids n_iter() int Iterations run in best init
Brute-force k-nearest neighbors with majority voting (classifier) or averaging (regressor). Mirrors sklearn.neighbors.KNeighborsClassifier.
Skigen::KNeighborsClassifier<Scalar> clf(int n_neighbors = 5); Skigen::KNeighborsRegressor<Scalar> reg(int n_neighbors = 5); Parameter Default Description n_neighbors 5 Number of neighbors to use
Method Description fit(X, y) Store training data predict(X) Predict class labels by majority vote score(X, y) Return accuracy score
Method Description fit(X, y) Store training data predict(X) Predict by averaging neighbors
#include <Skigen/Neighbors> Skigen::KNeighborsClassifier clf(3); clf.fit(X_train, y_train); std::cout << "Accuracy: " << clf.score(X_test, y_test) << "\n";
Encodes categorical labels to contiguous integer indices [0, n_classes). Mirrors sklearn.preprocessing.LabelEncoder.
Skigen::LabelEncoder<Label> encoder; Template Default Description Label int Label type
Method Description fit(y) Learn label-to-index mapping transform(y) Encode labels to integers fit_transform(y) Fit and transform in one call inverse_transform(y) Decode back to original labels
Accessor Type Description classes() std::vector<Label> Sorted unique classes n_classes() Eigen::Index Number of classes
#include <Skigen/Preprocessing> Skigen::LabelEncoder encoder; auto encoded = encoder.fit_transform(labels); auto decoded = encoder.inverse_transform(encoded);
L1-regularized linear regression using coordinate descent with soft-thresholding. Mirrors sklearn.linear_model.Lasso.

Skigen::Lasso<Scalar> model(Scalar alpha = 1, bool fit_intercept = true, int max_iter = 1000, Scalar tol = 1e-4); Parameter Default Description alpha 1 Regularization strength fit_intercept true Whether to calculate the intercept max_iter 1000 Maximum iterations tol 1e-4 Convergence tolerance
Method Description fit(X, y) Fit the model to training data predict(X) Predict using the linear model score(X, y) Return R² score
Accessor Type Description coef() RowVectorType Estimated coefficients (sparse) intercept() Scalar Intercept term
#include <Skigen/LinearModel> Skigen::Lasso model(/*alpha=*/0.1); model.fit(X, y); std::cout << "Non-zero coefs: " << (model.coef().array().abs() > 1e-10).count() << "\n";
Ordinary Least Squares linear regression via ColPivHouseholderQR decomposition. Mirrors sklearn.linear_model.LinearRegression.
Skigen::LinearRegression<Scalar> model(bool fit_intercept = true); Parameter Default Description fit_intercept true Whether to calculate the intercept
Method Description fit(X, y) Fit the model to training data predict(X) Predict using the linear model score(X, y) Return R² score
Accessor Type Description coef() RowVectorType Estimated coefficients intercept() Scalar Intercept term rank() IndexType Rank of the design matrix
L2-regularized logistic regression classifier with IRLS solver. Supports binary and multiclass (one-vs-rest) classification. Mirrors sklearn.linear_model.LogisticRegression.

Skigen::LogisticRegression<Scalar> model(Scalar C = 1, bool fit_intercept = true, int max_iter = 100, Scalar tol = 1e-4); Parameter Default Description C 1 Inverse regularization strength fit_intercept true Whether to calculate the intercept max_iter 100 Maximum IRLS iterations tol 1e-4 Convergence tolerance
Method Description fit(X, y) Fit the classifier to training data predict(X) Predict class labels predict_proba(X) Predict class probabilities score(X, y) Return accuracy score
Accessor Type Description coef() MatrixType Coefficient matrix intercept() VectorType Intercept vector classes() std::vector<int> Unique class labels

#include <Skigen/LinearModel> #include <Eigen/Dense> int main() { Eigen::MatrixXd X(6, 2); X << 1,2, 2,3, 3,4, 5,6, 6,7, 7,8; Eigen::VectorXi y(6); y << 0, 0, 0, 1, 1, 1; Skigen::LogisticRegression model(/*C=*/1.0); model.fit(X, y); auto predictions = model.predict(X); std::cout << "Accuracy: " << model.score(X, y) << "\n"; }
Feature scaling by dividing each feature by its maximum absolute value. Scales to [-1, 1] with optional clipping. Mirrors sklearn.preprocessing.MaxAbsScaler.
Skigen::MaxAbsScaler<Scalar> scaler(bool clip = false);
Method Description fit(X) Compute per-feature max absolute value transform(X) Scale to [-1, 1] inverse_transform(Z) Recover original scale transform_inplace(X) Scale in-place
Accessor Type Description max_abs() RowVectorType Per-feature max absolute value scale() RowVectorType Per-feature scale factor
#include <Skigen/Preprocessing> Skigen::MaxAbsScaler scaler; auto X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X);
Evaluation metrics for regression and classification tasks.

#include <Skigen/Metrics> Skigen::Metrics::mean_squared_error(y_true, y_pred); Skigen::Metrics::root_mean_squared_error(y_true, y_pred); Skigen::Metrics::mean_absolute_error(y_true, y_pred); Skigen::Metrics::r2_score(y_true, y_pred); Function Description mean_squared_error Mean of squared errors root_mean_squared_error Square root of MSE mean_absolute_error Mean of absolute errors r2_score Coefficient of determination

#include <Skigen/Metrics> Skigen::Metrics::accuracy_score(y_true, y_pred); Skigen::Metrics::precision_score(y_true, y_pred); Skigen::Metrics::recall_score(y_true, y_pred); Skigen::Metrics::f1_score(y_true, y_pred); auto cm = Skigen::Metrics::confusion_matrix(y_true, y_pred); Function Description accuracy_score Fraction of correct predictions precision_score Precision (positive predictive value) recall_score Recall (sensitivity) f1_score Harmonic mean of precision and recall confusion_matrix N×N confusion matrix

#include <Skigen/LinearModel> #include <Skigen/Metrics> #include <Skigen/ModelSelection> auto [X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test] = Skigen::train_test_split(X, y); Skigen::LinearRegression model; model.fit(X_train, y_train); auto y_pred = model.predict(X_test); std::cout << "MSE: " << Skigen::Metrics::mean_squared_error(y_test, y_pred) << "\n"; std::cout << "R²: " << Skigen::Metrics::r2_score(y_test, y_pred) << "\n";
Feature scaling to a specified range [min, max] with optional clipping. Mirrors sklearn.preprocessing.MinMaxScaler.
Skigen::MinMaxScaler<Scalar> scaler({min, max}, bool clip = false); Parameter Default Description feature_range {0, 1} Target range clip false Clip to range after transform

Method Description fit(X) Compute per-feature min and max transform(X) Scale to target range fit_transform(X) Fit and transform in one call inverse_transform(Z) Recover original scale transform_inplace(X) Scale in-place inverse_transform_inplace(Z) Recover in-place
Accessor Type Description data_min() RowVectorType Per-feature minimum data_max() RowVectorType Per-feature maximum scale() RowVectorType Per-feature scale factor
#include <Skigen/Preprocessing> Skigen::MinMaxScaler scaler({0, 1}); scaler.fit(X); auto X_scaled = scaler.transform(X); // All values now in [0, 1]
Utilities for splitting data and evaluating models.

Splits arrays into random train and test subsets. #include <Skigen/ModelSelection> auto [X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test] = Skigen::train_test_split( X, y, /*test_size=*/0.25, /*random_state=*/42, /*shuffle=*/true ); Parameter Default Description test_size 0.25 Fraction of data for testing random_state 42 Random seed shuffle true Whether to shuffle before splitting

K-fold cross-validation scoring. #include <Skigen/ModelSelection> Skigen::LinearRegression model; auto scores = Skigen::cross_val_score(model, X, y, /*cv=*/5); std::cout << "Mean R²: " << scores.mean() << "\n"; Parameter Default Description cv 5 Number of folds shuffle true Whether to shuffle before folding random_state 42 Random seed
Row-wise normalization to unit norm (L1, L2, or max). Stateless transformer. Mirrors sklearn.preprocessing.Normalizer.
Skigen::Normalizer<Scalar> normalizer(Norm norm = Norm::L2); Parameter Default Description norm L2 Norm to use: L1, L2, or Max
Method Description fit(X) No-op (stateless) transform(X) Normalize each row transform_inplace(X) Normalize in-place
#include <Skigen/Preprocessing> Skigen::Normalizer normalizer(Skigen::Normalizer<>::Norm::L2); auto X_normed = normalizer.fit_transform(X); // Each row now has unit L2 norm
Principal Component Analysis using full SVD with centering and explained variance computation. Mirrors sklearn.decomposition.PCA.
Skigen::PCA<Scalar> pca(Eigen::Index n_components = 0); Parameter Default Description n_components 0 Number of components to keep (0 = all)
Method Description fit(X) Fit the model with X transform(X) Apply dimensionality reduction fit_transform(X) Fit and transform in one call inverse_transform(X) Transform data back to original space

Accessor Type Description components() MatrixType Principal axes (rows) explained_variance() VectorType Variance per component explained_variance_ratio() VectorType Percentage of variance per component singular_values() VectorType Singular values mean() RowVectorType Per-feature mean

#include <Skigen/Decomposition> #include <Eigen/Dense> #include <iostream> int main() { Eigen::MatrixXd X = Eigen::MatrixXd::Random(100, 10); Skigen::PCA pca(3); // keep 3 components pca.fit(X); Eigen::MatrixXd X_reduced = pca.transform(X); // 100 x 3 std::cout << "Explained variance ratio: " << pca.explained_variance_ratio().transpose() << "\n"; }
Compile-time pipeline chaining transformers and a final estimator. All steps are type-checked at compile time. Mirrors sklearn.pipeline.Pipeline.
auto pipe = Skigen::make_pipeline(step1, step2, ..., estimator); Or explicitly: Skigen::Pipeline<StandardScaler<>, PCA<>, LinearRegression<>> pipe(scaler, pca, model);
Method Description fit(X, y) Fit all steps sequentially predict(X) Transform through all steps, then predict score(X, y) Transform and score get<I>() Access step at index I

#include <Skigen/Pipeline> #include <Skigen/Preprocessing> #include <Skigen/LinearModel> auto pipe = Skigen::make_pipeline( Skigen::StandardScaler<>(), Skigen::LinearRegression<>() ); pipe.fit(X_train, y_train); std::cout << "R²: " << pipe.score(X_test, y_test) << "\n"; // Access fitted scaler auto& scaler = pipe.get<0>(); std::cout << "Mean: " << scaler.mean() << "\n";
Generates polynomial and interaction features up to specified degree. Mirrors sklearn.preprocessing.PolynomialFeatures.

Skigen::PolynomialFeatures<Scalar> poly(int degree = 2, bool include_bias = true, bool interaction_only = false); Parameter Default Description degree 2 Maximum polynomial degree include_bias true Include a bias column of ones interaction_only false Only interaction features (no powers)
Method Description fit(X) Compute output feature structure transform(X) Generate polynomial features fit_transform(X) Fit and transform in one call
Accessor Type Description n_output_features() Eigen::Index Number of output features
#include <Skigen/Preprocessing> // X has 2 features → degree=2 generates: 1, x1, x2, x1², x1·x2, x2² Skigen::PolynomialFeatures poly(2); auto X_poly = poly.fit_transform(X);
L2-regularized least squares solved via Cholesky decomposition on the normal equation. Mirrors sklearn.linear_model.Ridge.
Skigen::Ridge<Scalar> model(Scalar alpha = 1, bool fit_intercept = true); Parameter Default Description alpha 1 Regularization strength fit_intercept true Whether to calculate the intercept
Method Description fit(X, y) Fit the model to training data predict(X) Predict using the linear model score(X, y) Return R² score
Accessor Type Description coef() RowVectorType Estimated coefficients intercept() Scalar Intercept term
#include <Skigen/LinearModel> Skigen::Ridge model(/*alpha=*/0.5); model.fit(X, y); auto predictions = model.predict(X_test);
Scaling using median centering and IQR-based scaling, robust to outliers. Mirrors sklearn.preprocessing.RobustScaler.

Skigen::RobustScaler<Scalar> scaler(bool with_centering = true, bool with_scaling = true, {q_min, q_max} = {25, 75}); Parameter Default Description with_centering true Center by median with_scaling true Scale by IQR quantile_range {25, 75} Quantile range for scaling
Method Description fit(X) Compute median and IQR transform(X) Scale using median and IQR inverse_transform(Z) Recover original scale transform_inplace(X) Scale in-place
Accessor Type Description center() RowVectorType Per-feature median scale() RowVectorType Per-feature IQR
#include <Skigen/Preprocessing> Skigen::RobustScaler scaler; scaler.fit(X); auto X_scaled = scaler.transform(X);
Stochastic Gradient Descent linear models for classification (Hinge or Log loss) and regression. Mirrors sklearn.linear_model.SGDClassifier.

Skigen::SGDClassifier<Scalar> clf( Loss loss = Loss::Hinge, Scalar alpha = 1e-4, int max_iter = 1000, Scalar tol = 1e-3, Scalar eta0 = 0.01, unsigned int random_state = 42); Skigen::SGDRegressor<Scalar> reg( Scalar alpha = 1e-4, int max_iter = 1000, Scalar tol = 1e-3, Scalar eta0 = 0.01, unsigned int random_state = 42);
Method Description fit(X, y) Fit model with SGD predict(X) Predict labels / values score(X, y) Accuracy (classifier) or R² (regressor)
#include <Skigen/LinearModel> Skigen::SGDClassifier clf(Skigen::SGDClassifier<>::Loss::Log); clf.fit(X_train, y_train); std::cout << "Accuracy: " << clf.score(X_test, y_test) << "\n";
Standardize features by removing the mean and scaling to unit variance. Zij=Xij−μjsjZ_{ij} = \frac{X_{ij} - \mu_j}{s_j}Zij​=sj​Xij​−μj​​ Mirrors sklearn.preprocessing.StandardScaler.
Skigen::StandardScaler<Scalar> scaler(bool with_mean = true, bool with_std = true); Parameter Default Description with_mean true Center data before scaling with_std true Scale data to unit variance

Method Description fit(X) Compute mean and standard deviation from X transform(X) Standardize X using fitted parameters fit_transform(X) Fit and transform in one call inverse_transform(Z) Recover original scale from standardized data transform_inplace(X) Standardize X in-place (zero allocation) inverse_transform_inplace(Z) Recover original scale in-place

Accessor Type Description mean() RowVectorType Per-feature mean var() RowVectorType Per-feature variance (ddof=0) scale() RowVectorType Per-feature scale factor (√var) n_samples_seen() IndexType Number of training samples n_features_in() IndexType Number of features

For maximum memory efficiency, use the in-place variants. These modify the input directly — no temporary allocation: Eigen::MatrixXd X = /* ... */; Skigen::StandardScaler scaler; scaler.fit(X); // Modifies X directly scaler.transform_inplace(X); // Recover scaler.inverse_transform_inplace(X);
Eigen::MatrixXf X = Eigen::MatrixXf::Random(1000, 100); Skigen::StandardScaler<float> scaler; Eigen::MatrixXf Z = scaler.fit_transform(X);

Features with variance below 10⋅ε10 \cdot \varepsilon10⋅ε (machine epsilon) are assigned a scale of 1.0, preventing division by near-zero values. This matches scikit-learn's _handle_zeros_in_scale() behavior.
Dimensionality reduction via truncated SVD without centering. Suitable for sparse matrices. Mirrors sklearn.decomposition.TruncatedSVD.
Skigen::TruncatedSVD<Scalar> svd(Eigen::Index n_components = 2); Parameter Default Description n_components 2 Number of components to keep
Method Description fit(X) Fit the model with X transform(X) Apply dimensionality reduction fit_transform(X) Fit and transform in one call inverse_transform(X) Transform data back to original space

Accessor Type Description components() MatrixType Components (rows) explained_variance() VectorType Variance per component explained_variance_ratio() VectorType Ratio of variance per component singular_values() VectorType Singular values
#include <Skigen/Decomposition> Skigen::TruncatedSVD svd(5); auto X_reduced = svd.fit_transform(X); // Project to 5 dimensions
